quotes.quote: Wes McKinney really is the original revolutionary that took this data wrangling tech and took it from the gods of Oracle and put it into the hands of everyone.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen traces the evolution of data architecture from the 1985 three-tier model through cloud data warehouses to a proposed inverted architecture where compute happens on client devices. He argues that the traditional top-down pyramid—where a central data warehouse holds all power—has barely changed despite moving to the cloud, and that modern single-node query engines like DuckDB combined with lakehouse formats can flip this model. He demonstrates DuckDB processing 6 billion rows on a laptop with only 2GB of memory, and shows examples of running analytics on 13-year-old laptops, phones, satellites, and cars. The core proposal is that clients should be empowered with local compute while sharing data through commodity storage and open metadata formats.
quotes.key_insight: The data architecture should be flipped upside down: instead of powerful central servers serving dumb clients, empowered client devices with local query engines should do the compute while sharing data through commodity storage and open lakehouse formats.

quotes.quote: There is this catalog up there that has a database, maybe we should use this for all.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen and Mark Raasveldt discuss DuckLake, their reimagination of lakehouse formats that replaces file-based metadata (JSON, Avro) with a SQL relational database for all metadata management while keeping data files on object stores. The project emerged from implementing Iceberg and Delta support in DuckDB and realizing the catalog database could handle everything more simply. DuckLake offers unique features like data inlining for small writes, file-level encryption with keys stored in the catalog, and cheap snapshots. The format scales from a single local DuckDB file to thousands of compute nodes, and can serve as a backend for the Iceberg REST catalog. They position it as DuckDB's multiplayer mode, fundamentally flipping the traditional architecture to make client-side compute the primary player.
quotes.key_insight: By replacing file-based metadata with a SQL database, DuckLake dramatically simplifies the lakehouse stack while enabling features like instant small writes, cheap snapshots, and file-level encryption that are difficult or impossible with existing formats.

quotes.quote: It takes this traditional two-tier architecture or three-tier architecture and flips it around where your client or your local machine is no longer the smallest light in the architecture, but it's suddenly actually the prominent player because it's where the compute happens.

quotes.quote: We just looked at that stack at some point, because we are obviously the people that have to implement this in the end, and at some point thought, hey, hang on, there is this catalog up there that has a database, maybe we should use this for all.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen and Mark Raasveldt introduce DuckLake, a reimagined lakehouse format that replaces file-based metadata (JSON, Avro) with a SQL relational database for all metadata management while keeping data files on object stores. The project emerged from critically examining the Iceberg and Delta tech stacks while implementing support in DuckDB, realizing the catalog database could handle everything. DuckLake dramatically reduces complexity, cutting query round trips from ~7 to 2, and enables features like data inlining (small writes go directly to the metadata store), per-file encryption with a single boolean flag, and cheap snapshots. The format scales from local single-file setups to thousands of distributed compute nodes, and can serve as a backend for Iceberg REST catalogs.
quotes.key_insight: By replacing file-based lakehouse metadata with a standard SQL database, DuckLake eliminates enormous complexity while preserving all the benefits of open table formats, enabling deployment scenarios from a single laptop to thousands of distributed nodes.

quotes.quote: We just looked at that stack at some point, because we are obviously the people that have to implement this in the end, because people kept asking us to make support for these things in DuckDB.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen and Mark Raasveldt discuss DuckLake, their reimagination of lakehouse formats that replaces file-based metadata (JSON, Avro) with a SQL relational database for metadata management while keeping data files on object stores. They argue this dramatically simplifies the lakehouse stack, reducing round trips from six or seven to two, and enabling features like data inlining for small writes, per-file encryption, and cheap snapshots. DuckLake scales from a single local DuckDB file up to thousands of compute nodes, and can serve as a backend for the Iceberg REST catalog. The project leverages DuckDB's existing infrastructure and has received overwhelmingly positive community response despite being only at version 0.2.
quotes.key_insight: Replacing file-based lakehouse metadata with a SQL database eliminates enormous complexity while preserving all the benefits of open table formats, and DuckDB's lightweight embeddability makes it a natural gateway for any engine to interact with this simpler architecture.

DuckLake scales from a single local DuckDB file up to thousands of compute nodes, and can serve as a backend for the Iceberg REST catalog. The project leverages DuckDB's existing infrastructure and has received overwhelmingly positive community response despite being only at version 0.2.
quotes.key_insight: Replacing file-based lakehouse metadata with a SQL database eliminates enormous complexity while preserving all the benefits of open table formats, and DuckDB's lightweight embeddability makes it a natural gateway for any engine to interact with this simpler architecture.

quotes.quote: We had tables 1,000 years before we had text. So this is a fundamental civilization technique that's older than the written text.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen argues that database abstractions—tables, SQL, and ACID—are fundamental civilizational technologies deserving of enthusiasm rather than dismissal. He traces tables back 5,000 years to Mesopotamian clay tablets, predating written text by a millennium. He contends that criticisms of databases conflate old implementations (like Oracle) with the underlying abstractions, which remain elegant and powerful. He uses MongoDB as a cautionary tale: by initially rejecting tables, SQL, and ACID, it forced developers to reimplement those concepts poorly in application code. He concludes with a technical demonstration of DuckDB's vectorized execution achieving near-theoretical hardware limits.
quotes.key_insight: The enduring abstractions of tables, SQL, and ACID are fundamental civilizational technologies whose elegance should not be confused with the limitations of their oldest implementations.

quotes.quote: We had tables 1,000 years before we had text. So this is a fundamental civilization technique that's older than the written text.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen argues that databases and their core abstractions—tables, SQL, and ACID—are fundamentally worthy of enthusiasm and affection, despite widespread criticism. He traces table technology back 5,000 years to Mesopotamian clay tablets, predating written text by a millennium, establishing tables as a foundational civilizational technique. He recounts how DuckDB was born from frustration with Spark's poor developer experience, aiming to make large dataset processing work simply on a laptop. He critiques MongoDB for abandoning these proven abstractions, noting that developers end up reimplementing schema validation, joins, and consistency checks by hand—and that MongoDB eventually added all three features back. He demonstrates DuckDB's vectorized execution achieving near-theoretical hardware limits, and concludes that the elegance of database abstractions should be judged separately from the clunkiness of legacy implementations.
quotes.key_insight: The core database abstractions of tables, SQL, and ACID are 5,000-year-old civilizational techniques that remain fundamentally sound—criticisms of databases are really criticisms of legacy implementations, not the abstractions themselves.

quotes.quote: You just told us you're doing all this stuff to not have a database. And now you're adding the database back, but you never go back to your original design decisions about not having a database and having all these files.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents DuckLake, a new lakehouse format that replaces Iceberg's complex file-based metadata architecture with a simple SQL database for catalog management. He traces the evolution from single-node databases through shared-nothing architectures to separated storage and compute, showing how Iceberg ironically reintroduced the database it tried to eliminate via its REST catalog. DuckLake acknowledges this reality by using a proper database for metadata alongside object storage for data files, eliminating layers of Avro and JSON metadata files. The result is dramatically simpler setup, fewer round trips, and 4x better performance than Iceberg in benchmarks.
quotes.key_insight: The lakehouse world spent years trying to eliminate databases from metadata management only to add them back via catalogs, proving that a simple SQL database for metadata plus object storage for data was always the right architecture.

quotes.quote: I will never understand the decision of having four different encodings for data in one file format. You have JSON, you have Avro, and you have Parquet, and you have the whole REST thing for the catalog. It's kind of crazy.

quotes.quote: You just told us you're doing all this stuff to not have a database. And now you're adding the database back, but you never go back to your original design decisions about not having a database and having all these files.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents DuckLake, a new lakehouse format that replaces Iceberg's complex multi-layer file-based metadata architecture with a simple SQL database for catalog storage. He traces the evolution from single-node databases through shared-nothing architectures to disaggregated storage-compute systems, arguing that Iceberg's attempt to eliminate the metadata database backfired when they had to re-add one via the REST catalog anyway. DuckLake stores all metadata (tables, columns, files, statistics) in a standard SQL database while keeping data files on object storage, reducing round trips from five sequential steps to two. Benchmarks show DuckLake is roughly four times faster than Iceberg in a localhost best-case scenario, with features like multi-table transactions, encryption, row inlining for small appends, and Iceberg import/export compatibility.
quotes.key_insight: The lakehouse movement's attempt to eliminate metadata databases was fundamentally misguided — acknowledging you need a real database for catalog metadata and removing the file-based complexity layers yields a simpler, faster, and more scalable architecture.

Benchmarks show DuckLake is roughly four times faster than Iceberg in a localhost best-case scenario, with features like multi-table transactions, encryption, row inlining for small appends, and Iceberg import/export compatibility.
quotes.key_insight: The lakehouse movement's attempt to eliminate metadata databases was fundamentally misguided — acknowledging you need a real database for catalog metadata and removing the file-based complexity layers yields a simpler, faster, and more scalable architecture.

quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen discusses DuckLake, a new open data format he and Mark Raasveldt created as an alternative to Iceberg and Delta Lake. He argues that Iceberg's fundamental problems are conceptual, not implementation-based, particularly the bolted-on catalog server that broke its original file-only design philosophy. DuckLake's key insight is using an existing database (like Postgres) to store all metadata—tables, columns, files, statistics—eliminating the need for Avro, JSON, REST APIs, and Java catalog servers. The approach enables sub-second query planning even at petabyte scale with 100 million snapshots, high-frequency transactional updates, and dramatically simpler implementation for connector authors.
quotes.key_insight: If you already need a database to manage your data lake catalog, you should redesign the entire metadata layer around that database rather than bolting one onto a file-based format that was designed to avoid needing one.

quotes.quote: I think Iceberg's problems are conceptual, fundamental, and like baked in the specification, which is an entirely different way of being wrong.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen discusses the motivations behind creating DuckLake, a new open data format that uses a standard database for metadata instead of the file-based approach taken by Iceberg and Delta Lake. He argues that Iceberg's addition of a catalog server on top of its file-based design was a fundamental departure that should have triggered a complete redesign. DuckLake simplifies the stack by eliminating roughly 15 technologies (Avro, REST APIs, Java catalog servers) and replacing them with SQL tables in any database plus Parquet files on blob storage. The format supports high-frequency updates, scales to petabyte-level tables with 100 million snapshots, and can be implemented by anyone who can talk to a database and read/write Parquet files.
quotes.key_insight: If you've already accepted that a lakehouse needs a database for its catalog, you should redesign the entire format around that database rather than bolting it on top of a file-based architecture.

quotes.quote: People sort of threw the whole tech stack out of the window and went with something else, which is, to me, isn't sort of a logical reaction.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen introduces DuckLake, an evolution of open table formats that replaces complex metadata layers (Avro files, JSON, REST APIs) with a standard SQL database for metadata management while keeping Parquet files on blob storage. He explains how examining Iceberg's specification revealed unnecessary complexity born from an emotional reaction to vendor lock-in rather than sound technical reasoning. DuckLake separates storage, compute, and metadata into three independently scalable axes, enabling features like cross-table commits, efficient small writes, per-file encryption, and time travel without the snapshot management overhead of existing formats. The system works with any SQL database supporting transactions and primary keys as the metadata store.
quotes.key_insight: Open table formats unnecessarily reinvented metadata management outside databases due to vendor lock-in fears, and DuckLake corrects this by putting metadata back where it belongs—in a SQL database—while keeping the good parts of decoupled blob storage.

quotes.quote: The crazy thing is that these formats, they were built to work with files and blob stores, and that's great. And then they realized they couldn't do it, and they added the Postgres to it in the form of the catalog server.

quotes.quote: The first thing, you know, I sat down, went to the website, looked at the specification. I was like, OK, I can do this. I'm a medium-competent database developer. I can implement Iceberg in DuckDB. And so we're like, OK, right, you read the JSON file. OK, great. We can do that. Then it's like, Avro, what?
quotes.tone: opinionated, candid, technically grounded, and irreverently practical
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents DuckLake, a new open table format that replaces the complex metadata layers of Iceberg (Avro files, JSON manifests, REST catalogs) with a simple SQL database for metadata storage while keeping Parquet files on blob storage. The idea originated from his frustration implementing Iceberg support in DuckDB, where he found the format's complexity to be an overengineered solution to what is fundamentally an economic problem with vendor lock-in. DuckLake stores all metadata in any SQL database (Postgres, DuckDB, etc.) and data files on any blob storage, enabling cross-table commits, efficient small writes, per-file encryption, and virtually unlimited snapshots. He demonstrates the system working with both local DuckDB files and remote Postgres+S3 configurations, showing time travel, deletes, updates, and schema changes. The format reuses Iceberg's Parquet-based delete file format and plans zero-copy import/export with Iceberg for interoperability.
quotes.key_insight: Open table formats like Iceberg overcomplicated metadata management by avoiding databases, and DuckLake fixes this by simply putting the metadata back in a SQL database where it belongs while keeping the good parts — Parquet files on blob storage.

quotes.quote: They tried so hard not to have a database in this because databases are evil. They don't scale.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen and Mark Raasveldt introduce DuckLake, a new lakehouse format that replaces the file-based metadata layers of Iceberg and Delta with a standard database (like Postgres or DuckDB) for metadata storage while keeping Parquet files on blob storage for data. They argue that existing lakehouse formats made poor design decisions by trying to avoid databases, only to eventually require catalog servers backed by databases anyway. DuckLake simplifies the architecture to just two components—a metadata database and file storage—enabling multi-table transactions, fast small commits, data inlining, and zero-trust encryption. The format is an open standard using simple SQL, and the DuckDB extension implementation is MIT-licensed.
quotes.key_insight: Since every lakehouse format already requires a database for its catalog server, the logical step is to store all metadata directly in that database, eliminating the complex file-based metadata layers and unlocking multi-table ACID transactions, fast small commits, and zero-trust encryption.

quotes.quote: In order to solve the problem of multi-table management and atomic commits, we'll invent a new system and this is no longer going to be file-based. It's going to be a service. And how does it store that? Well, it uses a system that is specifically designed to manage data. A database management system, if you will.

quotes.quote: They tried so hard not to have a database in this because databases are evil. They don't scale.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen and Mark Raasveldt introduce DuckLake, a new lakehouse format that replaces the complex file-based metadata layers of Iceberg and Delta with a standard database (like Postgres or DuckDB) for metadata storage while keeping Parquet files on blob storage for data. They argue that existing lakehouse formats like Iceberg were built on the Hadoop-era premise that databases don't scale, yet ironically ended up requiring a database (Postgres) as a catalog server anyway, without revisiting their original file-based design decisions. DuckLake's three principles are simplicity (just a database plus file storage), scalability (metadata is ~100,000x smaller than data, and the architecture mirrors proven designs from Snowflake and BigQuery), and speed (single round-trip queries instead of multiple file hops). The format enables features impossible in existing lakehouses: multi-table transactions, data inlining for small writes, zero-trust encryption via Parquet encryption with keys in the metadata store, and consistent catalog-level snapshots. DuckLake serves as DuckDB's answer to multiplayer/multi-user scenarios without implementing a traditional client-server protocol.
quotes.key_insight: Lakehouse formats like Iceberg already require a database server for their catalog, so DuckLake simply uses that database for all metadata instead of maintaining redundant layers of Avro and JSON files, achieving the same proven architecture as Snowflake and BigQuery but with fully open formats.

quotes.quote: We can't just pip import the state of the art in data. We might have to build this ourselves.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen recounts the origin story of DuckDB, born from frustration with the state of analytical data management in 2015, when options were expensive enterprise systems, Hadoop, immature cloud services, or bolted-on columnar extensions. He and Mark Raasveldt decided to build 'the SQLite for analytics' at CWI Amsterdam, making key design decisions: single-node over distributed, vectorized execution over JIT compilation, CPU-only with no GPU support, no hardware-specific SIMD code, and MIT-style open source licensing. He demonstrates DuckDB's out-of-core hash join capabilities and argues that the embedded architecture liberates data management by allowing DuckDB to run anywhere from browsers to Raspberry Pis to outer space.
quotes.key_insight: The most impactful design decisions in DuckDB were acts of deliberate simplification — single-node, no GPU, no SIMD, no JIT — which maximized portability and enabled analytics to run anywhere, from browsers to Raspberry Pis.

quotes.key_insight: The most impactful design decisions in DuckDB were acts of deliberate simplification — single-node, no GPU, no SIMD, no JIT — which maximized portability and enabled analytics to run anywhere, from browsers to Raspberry Pis.

quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen traces the origin story of DuckDB back to 2015, when analytical data management was fragmented between expensive enterprise systems, Hadoop, immature cloud offerings, and academic spinoffs that got swallowed by corporations. He and Mark Raasveldt decided to build 'the SQLite for analytics' — a single-node, embedded, vectorized, CPU-only, open-source database. He walks through key design decisions (no distribution, no GPU, no SIMD, MIT license) and presents recent work on out-of-core hash joins that gracefully handle memory limits instead of crashing. He concludes by showing DuckDB running TPC-H at massive scale factors on commodity hardware and teases a new product launching soon.
quotes.key_insight: The most impactful design decisions in DuckDB were the ones that defied conventional wisdom — choosing single-node over distributed, vectorization over JIT, CPU over GPU, and open source over proprietary — and each has been validated by real-world adoption.

quotes.quote: What you can do on a single computer is insane. You would not believe it if you wouldn't see it.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen traces the evolution of data architecture from the 1985 three-tier model through cloud data warehouses to a new client-empowered paradigm. He argues that the traditional top-down architecture, where a central data warehouse holds all power, has barely changed despite moving to the cloud. Demonstrating that modern hardware can handle surprisingly large datasets on single machines, he makes the case that compute should move to the edge. He proposes flipping the architecture so clients do their own processing while lakehouse formats and commodity storage provide shared coordination.
quotes.key_insight: The future of data architecture is flipping the pyramid — empowering edge devices with capable local query engines while using commodity lakehouse formats for coordination, eliminating the need for expensive centralized data warehouses.

quotes.quote: I see AI on the same level of idiocy as Bitcoin personally.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen discusses DuckDB's origins, philosophy, and growth on The Data Engineering Show. He explains how DuckDB emerged from CWI research as the first in-process analytical database, designed for universal deployment from phones to space satellites. He emphasizes DuckDB's focus on portability over hardware-specific optimization, explains why they built their own Parquet reader, and shares strong skepticism about AI's role in database internals while acknowledging its value for user-facing assistance like text-to-SQL.
quotes.key_insight: DuckDB's power comes from radical simplicity and portability—avoiding hardware-specific optimizations and AI hype in favor of being a universal, embeddable database component that works everywhere.

quotes.quote: I see AI on the same level of idiocy as Bitcoin personally.
quotes.summary: In this podcast interview, Hannes Mühleisen discusses DuckDB's origins at CWI, its growth to 10 million monthly downloads, and its design philosophy as a universal data wrangling tool. He explains why DuckDB avoids hardware-specific optimizations in favor of maximum portability, contrasting this with SaaS-oriented systems like ClickHouse. Hannes shares the story of building DuckDB's parquet reader from scratch due to the lack of a standalone C++ component, and argues that a competent parquet reader inevitably becomes a database engine. He is bluntly skeptical of AI in database internals, comparing it to Bitcoin in usefulness, while acknowledging AI's role in assisting users with SQL generation. He highlights DuckDB's extension ecosystem as a key growth driver that lets subcultures build what they need on top of DuckDB's infrastructure.
quotes.key_insight: DuckDB's power comes from ruthlessly prioritizing portability and simplicity as a universal component, resisting the temptation to chase hardware-specific optimizations or AI integration that would compromise its SQLite-like ubiquity.

quotes.quote: The intuition is really to move from this world where people generally fear large data sets because they make everything more complicated to a world where you are no longer scared of this big pile of CSV files.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen opens DuckCon #6 in Amsterdam, reaffirming DuckDB's mission to make large datasets approachable rather than intimidating. He presents evidence that single-node computing power has outpaced the growth of useful data sizes, citing analysis showing P99.9 of data warehouse sizes is under 300GB. He shares adoption metrics including 32 million monthly extension installs and 1.8 million monthly website visitors. The talk transitions to Mark discussing upcoming v1.2 features and the lakehouse focus for the year ahead, followed by Q&A covering architecture, Wasm, partitioning, and extension development.
quotes.key_insight: The era of big data infrastructure is largely unnecessary for most workloads, as single-node hardware with efficient software like DuckDB can handle 99.9% of real-world analytical datasets.

quotes.quote: We saw people struggling with anything that didn't fit in Excel. And then the tools available at the time were just not nice or not instilling confidence.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen opens DuckCon #6 in Amsterdam, reflecting on DuckDB's mission to eliminate fear of large datasets by making single-node analytics powerful and accessible. He presents data showing that 99.9% of analytical workloads fit on a single machine, citing evidence that the P99.9 of data set sizes in Snowflake and Redshift is under 300GB. DuckDB's adoption metrics are staggering: 32 million extension installs per month, 1.8 million unique web visitors monthly. The team previews DuckDB v1.2 (Harlequin), announces lakehouse support as the major 2025 focus, and discusses plans to make the extension ecosystem more accessible through a C API that enables extensions in Rust and Go.
quotes.key_insight: The 'data singularity' has arrived: single-node hardware capability has outpaced the growth of real-world datasets, making distributed systems unnecessary for 99.9% of analytical workloads.

quotes.quote: I find it always funny when people sit in front of a $3,000 sort of Mac book that runs a web browser and nothing else. And then they use that to spend more money on Snowflake in order to query like a bunch of rows.
quotes.summary: In this Posit Data Science Hangout, Hannes Mühleisen discusses DuckDB's origins, architecture, and growing adoption. He explains how DuckDB emerged from frustrations teaching Spark courses and conversations with the R community about real data problems. He emphasizes that DuckDB's in-process architecture eliminates client-server complexity, runs anywhere from laptops to browsers, and handles mid-range data that previously required big data solutions. He addresses corporate adoption, noting DuckDB is widely embedded in BI tools and data platforms, and discusses the project's culture of friendliness and controlled governance.
quotes.key_insight: DuckDB's success came from observing that most data practitioners were struggling with unnecessarily complex tools, and that a simple in-process database could replace distributed systems for the vast majority of real-world analytical workloads.

quotes.quote: We were initially ridiculed for not having a distributed system. And it was our strong belief, and hill we were willing to die on, that you don't actually need distributed systems in order to query significant amounts of data.

quotes.summary: In this Posit Data Science Hangout, Hannes Mühleisen discusses DuckDB's origins, architecture, and growing adoption. He explains how DuckDB emerged from frustrations teaching Spark courses and observing real data problems in the R community, evolving into an in-process analytical database that runs anywhere. He addresses questions about corporate adoption, security, database mental models, and DuckDB's deliberate focus on relational operations rather than expanding into machine learning. Hannes also discusses DuckDB's open-source governance model, where the original creators maintain directional control to avoid the fate of unfocused Apache projects.
quotes.key_insight: DuckDB's success came from observing real user struggles with overcomplicated data tools and betting that most analytical workloads don't need distributed systems — just a well-engineered in-process engine on hardware people already own.

quotes.quote: What if we take the body of knowledge, the orthodoxy of what the database engine should look like and just put it into a package that doesn't make you hate everything and everyone around you?
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen discusses DuckDB's origins, philosophy, and explosive growth as an embedded analytical database. He explains why DuckDB prioritizes user experience, zero-dependency installation, and state-of-the-art query processing in a simple package. He critiques Lakehouse formats as technically inferior recreations of data warehouse features driven by market forces rather than technical merit. He argues the industry has been solving 'whale problems' while neglecting the 99% of users whose data fits on a single node, and advocates for transactional semantics in analytics without performance penalties.
quotes.key_insight: The database industry wasted a decade solving problems only tech giants have, while the real opportunity was packaging proven database technology into something ordinary users could actually install and use.

quotes.quote: We are essentially building things that are inferior to the state of the art from 20 years ago. And somehow get excited about it.

He critiques Lakehouse formats as technically inferior recreations of data warehouse features driven by market forces rather than technical merit. He argues the industry has been solving 'whale problems' while neglecting the 99% of users whose data fits on a single node, and advocates for transactional semantics in analytics without performance penalties.
quotes.key_insight: The database industry wasted a decade solving problems only tech giants have, while the real opportunity was packaging proven database technology into something ordinary users could actually install and use.

quotes.quote: Raw performance is overrated. You need to solve a problem. You're not solving the problem by being 2% faster and then the user not being able to install it.

quotes.quote: What if we take the body of knowledge, the orthodoxy of what the database engine should look like and just put it into a package that doesn't make you hate everything and everyone around you?
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen discusses DuckDB's philosophy of making database technology accessible without sacrificing correctness. He explains how DuckDB prioritizes user experience, zero-dependency installation, and state-of-the-art query processing in a single embeddable library. He critiques lakehouse formats as technically inferior reimplementations of data warehouse features driven by market forces rather than engineering merit. He argues that the industry has been solving 'whale problems' for the 99% of users who don't need distributed systems, and that single-node performance on modern hardware is sufficient for most real-world workloads. He also details DuckDB's innovations in larger-than-memory processing, ACID transactions for analytics, and the extension ecosystem.
quotes.key_insight: The database industry has been solving the wrong problems for the wrong users, and DuckDB's success comes from packaging proven database technology into an accessible form for the 99% of users who never needed distributed systems.

quotes.quote: Anything that stops fitting in Excel, people are terrified.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents DuckDB's ACID transaction capabilities as a solution to common data reliability problems in the Python ecosystem. He argues that data practitioners live in fear of their data because tools like pandas lack transactional guarantees, leading to issues like accidental data loss, partial file writes, and race conditions. He walks through each ACID property (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) with practical analytics examples, showing how DuckDB's in-process architecture combined with full transactional support lets analysts change data with confidence. He demonstrates that DuckDB's analytics-optimized MVCC delivers these guarantees without sacrificing performance.
quotes.key_insight: ACID transactions, long considered unnecessary overhead for analytics, are essential for data confidence—and DuckDB proves they can be delivered without performance compromise in an embedded, zero-dependency package.

quotes.quote: Anything that stops fitting in Excel, people are terrified. It's my experience after working with data people for a while.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents DuckDB's ACID transaction capabilities as a solution to common data reliability problems in the Python data stack. He argues that DataFrames lack transactionality, leading to data loss from accidental overwrites, partial file writes, and race conditions. He walks through each ACID property—atomicity, consistency, isolation, durability—with practical analytics examples showing how DuckDB protects users from these pitfalls. He demonstrates that DuckDB's analytics-optimized MVCC implementation delivers transactional safety without the performance penalties traditionally associated with ACID compliance, making it both safer and faster than alternatives.
quotes.key_insight: ACID transactions, long considered unnecessary overhead for analytics, are essential for data reliability—and DuckDB proves they don't have to cost performance.

quotes.quote: Tables are a civilization technique that is older than writing things down.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen argues that the era of big data requiring distributed systems is effectively over, as research from Redshift and Snowflake shows median query scans use only 100MB and P99.9 scans fit under 300GB. This means single-node systems like DuckDB can handle virtually all analytical workloads. With smaller effective data sizes, we can reclaim ACID transaction properties that were sacrificed during the Hadoop era. He demonstrates how atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability bring real value to analytics pipelines. He also expresses skepticism about Lakehouse formats like Iceberg, viewing them as limited workarounds for change management on object stores.
quotes.key_insight: Since hardware growth is outpacing data size growth and most analytical queries scan under 300GB, single-node ACID-compliant databases can replace both distributed big data systems and Lakehouse format workarounds.

quotes.quote: Tables are a civilization technique that is older than writing things down.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen argues that the era of big data requiring distributed systems is effectively over, as research from Redshift and Snowflake shows the median analytics query scans only 100MB of data. He demonstrates that single-node hardware capabilities are growing faster than data processing demands, meaning most analytical workloads fit comfortably on a single machine. With smaller effective data sizes, he advocates for bringing back ACID transaction properties to analytics workflows, showing how atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability solve real problems in data pipelines. He closes with skepticism toward Lakehouse formats like Iceberg, arguing they are limited reimplementations of features that dedicated database systems already handle better.
quotes.key_insight: With the median analytics query scanning only 100MB, the distributed big data era's tradeoffs are obsolete — single-node systems can now deliver both scale and full ACID transactional guarantees for analytics.

quotes.key_insight: With the median analytics query scanning only 100MB, the distributed big data era's tradeoffs are obsolete — single-node systems can now deliver both scale and full ACID transactional guarantees for analytics.

quotes.quote: The fundamental hill that we were willing to die on was to bet on single node and scale up instead of scale out.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen discusses how DuckDB was built as a radical bet on single-node, in-process analytics over distributed scale-out architectures. Born from research at CWI Amsterdam, DuckDB combined state-of-the-art vectorized query execution with SQLite-like ease of use, after an earlier experiment (MonetDB Lite) proved the in-process model had demand. The 1.0 release focused on storage format stability and backward compatibility. Hannes explains how DuckDB's SQL dialect innovations like GROUP BY ALL have been widely copied, and discusses the emerging lakehouse ecosystem with mixed feelings about potential re-centralization of data access behind APIs.
quotes.key_insight: By refusing to distribute and betting everything on single-node performance, DuckDB exploited algorithmic advantages unavailable to distributed systems and captured the 99% of users who never needed Google-scale infrastructure.

quotes.quote: What we set out to do with DuckDB is to somehow bring the orthodoxy of databases, features like transactions, like schemas, like state-of-the-art execution engines together with sort of this ease of use that we saw with systems like Mongo.

quotes.quote: The fundamental hill that we were willing to die on was to bet on single node and scale up instead of scale out.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen explains how DuckDB emerged from frustration with poor database user experiences, combining state-of-the-art analytical engine research from CWI Amsterdam with SQLite-like simplicity. The fundamental bet was on single-node scale-up over distributed scale-out, which the entire field considered heretical at the time. He traces DuckDB's origins from MonetDB Lite experiments through the decision to start from scratch, enabled by his tenure security and Mark Raasveldt's flexible PhD timeline. The 1.0 release centered on storage format stability, and DuckDB's SQL dialect innovations like GROUP BY ALL have been widely copied by Snowflake and Databricks. Mühleisen expresses mixed feelings about lakehouse formats, noting that while they theoretically open walled gardens, API gatekeepers may recreate lock-in.
quotes.key_insight: By refusing to chase distributed scale-out and instead making an uncompromising bet on single-node performance, DuckDB gained algorithmic advantages that distributed engines structurally cannot match, while hardware trends validated that most real-world workloads never needed scale-out in the first place.

quotes.quote: What fundamentally DuckDB is about is to make people stop worrying about data.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents how DuckDB, an in-process analytical database, is being used in unexpected ways beyond its original interactive analysis purpose. He explains DuckDB's core design philosophy of end-to-end user experience and 'data confidence' — removing the terror of encountering messy datasets. He highlights three surprising deployment patterns: DuckDB as a pipeline component (e.g., Fivetran, Lambda functions), DuckDB on application servers replacing separate analytics infrastructure, and DuckDB on clients via desktop apps, mobile, and WebAssembly in browsers. His central thesis is that DuckDB liberates data processing from centralized server architectures, enabling data engines to run anywhere.
quotes.key_insight: The future of data processing is not centralized server architectures but embeddable engines that can run anywhere — in pipelines, application servers, browsers, and mobile devices — eliminating the friction and scaling problems of traditional data infrastructure.

quotes.quote: What fundamentally DuckDB is about is to make people stop worrying about data.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents how DuckDB has evolved beyond its original interactive analysis use case into unexpected deployment scenarios. He explains DuckDB's core design philosophy of eliminating data anxiety through an in-process, batteries-included approach that prioritizes end-to-end user experience over narrow engine optimization. The talk catalogs three emerging deployment patterns: DuckDB as a pipeline component (e.g., in ETL between operational databases and data lakes), as a Lambda function replacement for Spark clusters, and as a client-side engine in browsers, desktop apps, and mobile devices. Mühleisen argues this represents a fundamental shift where data engines can now live anywhere rather than being trapped in centralized server architectures.
quotes.key_insight: The era of centralized database servers is giving way to embedded, in-process data engines that can be deployed anywhere — in pipelines, Lambda functions, browsers, and mobile apps — fundamentally changing how we architect data systems.

quotes.key_insight: The era of centralized database servers is giving way to embedded, in-process data engines that can be deployed anywhere — in pipelines, Lambda functions, browsers, and mobile apps — fundamentally changing how we architect data systems.

quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents how DuckDB can be deployed at every tier of a data architecture, replacing the traditional centralized database server model. He argues that DuckDB's in-process, embeddable design allows it to run inside operational servers (via PG-DuckDB), in serverless Lambdas replacing Spark clusters, within app servers eliminating database protocol overhead, and even in browsers via WebAssembly. He frames this as a paradigm shift: state-of-the-art query processing is no longer bound to a fixed server or cluster but can live anywhere creativity allows.
quotes.key_insight: DuckDB's in-process architecture represents a paradigm shift where state-of-the-art analytical query processing is unshackled from centralized servers and can be embedded at every tier of a data architecture, from operational databases to browsers.

quotes.quote: People generally fear data. It's my observation, like, especially when it stops fitting in Excel.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents DuckDB's potential to disrupt traditional two-tier and three-tier data architectures by embedding the query engine at every layer of the stack. He argues that DuckDB's in-process architecture eliminates the need for centralized database servers, enabling deployment in operational servers, app servers, Lambdas, browsers, and mobile devices. He demonstrates how companies like Fivetran, Oktar, and Hugging Face are already using DuckDB to replace heavyweight infrastructure like Spark clusters. The core thesis is that unshackling a state-of-the-art query engine from fixed servers represents a massive paradigm shift in data architecture.
quotes.key_insight: The paradigm shift from centralized database servers to an embeddable, state-of-the-art query engine that can run anywhere—browsers, Lambdas, mobile apps, operational servers—fundamentally changes how data architectures should be designed.

quotes.quote: People generally fear data. Data sets, they can be unruly. You're gonna have that wrong comma in line 400,000 or something like that.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen opens DuckCon #5 by reflecting on DuckDB's mission and extraordinary growth. He frames DuckDB's core purpose as replacing fear of data with confidence, comparing the goal to how VLC made video files approachable. He outlines three main use cases: interactive analysis, pipeline components, and creative architectures like mobile devices and satellites. Adoption metrics are staggering — 6 million Python downloads per month, 17 million extension installs, and 600K unique web visitors. He emphasizes DuckDB is not VC-funded, sustained instead by commercial support contracts through DuckDB Labs.
quotes.key_insight: DuckDB's core mission is replacing the fear and hostility people experience with data tools by making data as approachable as opening a movie file in VLC.

quotes.quote: The tools people generally are using to look at data, or to wrangle with data, they tended to be, I think, hostile is the right word.

quotes.quote: People generally fear data. Data sets can be unruly. You're gonna have that wrong comma in line 400,000 or something like that.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen opens DuckCon #5 with a state-of-the-duck overview, describing DuckDB's mission to replace fear of data with confidence through simple, dependency-free tooling. He outlines three emerging use cases: interactive analysis, pipeline components, and creative architectures (mobile, browser, Lambda, satellites). Adoption metrics are staggering—6 million Python downloads/month (up from 2M in January), 17 million extension installs/month, and 600K unique web visitors. Mark Raasveldt then previews DuckDB 1.1, highlighting community extensions, dynamic SQL improvements, parallel streaming queries, and dynamic filter pushdown from joins. The team emphasizes that DuckDB Labs funds development through commercial support contracts rather than VC funding, and that post-1.0 focus is shifting toward extensions and the ecosystem edge rather than core bloat.
quotes.key_insight: DuckDB's explosive adoption stems from replacing hostile, complex data tooling with an embeddable system so simple that data becomes as approachable as opening a movie file.

quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen opens DuckCon #5 with a state-of-the-duck overview, describing DuckDB's mission to replace fear of data with confidence through simple, dependency-free tooling. He outlines three emerging use cases: interactive analysis, pipeline components, and creative architectures (mobile, browser, Lambda, satellites). Adoption metrics are staggering—6 million Python downloads/month (up from 2M in January), 17 million extension installs/month, and 600K unique web visitors. Mark Raasveldt then previews DuckDB 1.1, highlighting community extensions, dynamic SQL improvements, parallel streaming queries, and dynamic filter pushdown from joins. The team emphasizes that DuckDB Labs funds development through commercial support contracts rather than VC funding, and that post-1.0 focus is shifting toward extensions and the ecosystem edge rather than core bloat.
quotes.key_insight: DuckDB's explosive adoption stems from replacing hostile, complex data tooling with an embeddable system so simple that data becomes as approachable as opening a movie file.

quotes.quote: In the past you couldn't just stick a Spark cluster into a pipeline. That doesn't work. But with DuckDB you can just spin up an instance and run some minor transformation. That's perfectly fine.

quotes.quote: One of the cardinal sins people do with data systems is they have a columnar system and then they slap an old school row-wise API on top, like the Postgres API and protocol.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen discusses DuckDB's design philosophy, inspired by SQLite's ease of use but built for analytical workloads. He explains why DuckDB was written in C++, how it can query data directly from CSV and Parquet files without ingestion, and its three main use cases: prototyping, local data analysis, and enterprise data pipelines. He describes the technical challenges of building a database from scratch over six years, including transactions for columnar bulk updates, graceful memory degradation, and storage format backwards compatibility. He also discusses DuckDB Labs' unusual self-funded business model and the future vision of DuckDB as an extensible platform.
quotes.key_insight: By focusing on the 'tail scale' — solving data problems for the 99% who don't need distributed systems — DuckDB delivers a nimble, zero-configuration analytical engine that queries data where it lives without ingestion.

quotes.quote: We set out to solve the data problems for the 99% of us, because it's so common to sort of solve the problems of the one percenters.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen discusses DuckDB's origins, design philosophy, and technical architecture on Software Engineering Daily. He explains how DuckDB drew inspiration from SQLite's ease of use while swapping in an analytical query engine designed for columnar workloads. The conversation covers why C++11 was chosen over C or newer languages, DuckDB's three main use cases (prototyping, local data analysis, enterprise pipeline components), and its ability to query directly on external file formats like Parquet and CSV without ingestion. Hannes describes the 'tail scale' philosophy of solving data problems for the 99% rather than the 1%, and explains DuckDB Labs' bootstrapped business model funded by consulting and support rather than VC money.
quotes.key_insight: By applying SQLite's in-process simplicity to analytical workloads and enabling direct queries on external file formats, DuckDB solves the data problems of the 99% who don't need distributed computing.

quotes.quote: DuckDB is really perfect for this last mile of data analysis, where you may not need a whole data center to compute something.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen announces official DuckDB support for Delta Lake and the Unity Catalog at the Data + AI Summit 2024. He positions DuckDB as filling the gap between Excel and Spark for last-mile data analysis. The Delta Lake integration leverages Databricks' delta kernel rather than building from scratch. He also announces DuckDB community extensions, allowing anyone to publish and share extensions easily.
quotes.key_insight: DuckDB's Delta Lake integration via the shared delta kernel exemplifies how open ecosystem collaboration reduces duplicated effort and benefits everyone, especially small teams building in-process analytics tools.

quotes.quote: Tables were there before our civilization, and they will be there after our civilization.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen traces data management from 5,000-year-old Sumerian clay tablets to modern cloud databases, arguing that tables, SQL, and ACID transactions form an unbreakable 'holy trifecta.' He recounts how the NoSQL era saw companies abandon SQL for MapReduce due to hardware constraints, only to bring it back within years—MongoDB added schemas, Cassandra added ACID, and Databricks restored integrated storage. He predicts relational systems will absorb key-value stores, document databases, time series, vector databases, and graph databases, since all reduce to trivial table representations. Finally, he argues 'big data is dead' because modern single-node hardware is so powerful that distributing computation often makes things slower.
quotes.key_insight: The history of data management is a recurring cycle of abandoning and rediscovering the same fundamentals—tables, SQL, and ACID—and modern hardware has made distributed systems unnecessary for most workloads.

quotes.quote: There's this holy trifecta: Tables, SQL, ACID. The alternative is to have app developers do this themselves. And having app developers do this themselves is really not a great idea.

quotes.quote: Nobody really cared about the end-to-end user experience. Like what is this actually like to use a database system?
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen traces DuckDB's origin from CWI, the Dutch National Research Lab where Python was also invented, explaining how academic environments can be both safe spaces and traps for software development. He describes DuckDB's core philosophy: an in-process, zero-dependency analytical database that prioritizes the full user experience over just the query engine internals. He draws parallels to Python's origin story at CWI, including similar institutional tensions when research software gains real-world traction. The talk concludes with hard-won lessons about funding open-source companies, arguing for bootstrapping over venture capital to maintain project integrity.
quotes.key_insight: Building serious software in academia requires gambling your career on impact over papers, and sustaining it requires escaping institutional constraints through bootstrapping rather than surrendering control to venture capital.

quotes.quote: In a shocking departure from normal researcher behavior, we actually went out to talk to people in data analysis, and we found out that they hated databases.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen shares the story of DuckDB's creation at the CWI research institute in the Netherlands, drawing parallels to Python's origin at the same institution. He explains DuckDB's core philosophy of simplicity and in-process architecture, inspired by SQLite's success, and details how it bridges the gap between academic database research and practical usability. He candidly discusses the tensions of building production software in academia — the safe space for prototyping versus the career risks of not publishing papers — and how the team eventually had to leave CWI to form DuckDB Labs. He advocates for bootstrapping over venture capital funding, arguing that VC money inevitably leads to license changes or project bastardization, and explains how DuckDB Labs has been profitable from day one through support contracts.
quotes.key_insight: Academia is an ideal incubator for early-stage software projects, but sustaining and scaling them requires escaping to a self-funded business model that preserves project independence from both institutional constraints and venture capital pressure.

quotes.quote: We realized that nobody had really considered the ergonomics of using data management systems. It was always very complicated to set up.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen explains the technical foundations of DuckDB, an in-process analytical database built on vectorized query execution and morsel-driven parallelism. He describes how DuckDB originated from academic research at CWI Amsterdam, driven by the observation that no one had designed a data management system prioritizing usability and ease of data import/export. The conversation covers how vectorized execution processes data in 2,048-row column-oriented chunks to stay in CPU cache, and how morsel-driven parallelism improves on older exchange-based approaches by making operators parallelism-aware and reactive. Hannes argues that most data problems don't need distributed systems since hardware scales faster than human-generated data, and criticizes academia for solving 'whale problems' instead of helping the 99% of practitioners stuck on tools like Pandas.
quotes.key_insight: By starting from usability first principles rather than traditional database architecture, DuckDB arrived at an in-process, vectorized, morsel-parallel design that brings state-of-the-art academic database research to the 99% of data practitioners underserved by existing tools.

quotes.quote: We combine this crazy academic sort of knowledge on how data systems should be built, all wrapped into a package that tries to be friendly. And that's something that I don't think has been done before.

The conversation covers how vectorized execution processes data in 2,048-row column-oriented chunks to stay in CPU cache, and how morsel-driven parallelism improves on older exchange-based approaches by making operators parallelism-aware and reactive. Hannes argues that most data problems don't need distributed systems since hardware scales faster than human-generated data, and criticizes academia for solving 'whale problems' instead of helping the 99% of practitioners stuck on tools like Pandas.
quotes.key_insight: By starting from usability first principles rather than traditional database architecture, DuckDB arrived at an in-process, vectorized, morsel-parallel design that brings state-of-the-art academic database research to the 99% of data practitioners underserved by existing tools.

quotes.quote: We really designed this thing to be useful. So that was our academic angle.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen explains the technical foundations of DuckDB in this Developer Voices podcast, focusing on two key innovations: vectorized query execution and morsel-driven parallelism. He describes how DuckDB processes data in chunks of 2,048 rows to keep computations in CPU cache, achieving roughly 100x speedups over naive approaches. He contrasts this with exchange-based parallelism used by systems like Spark, which suffers from skew and inflexibility. Mühleisen also discusses DuckDB's origins as an academic project at CWI Amsterdam that prioritized usability and practical impact over theoretical novelty, arguing that most real-world data problems fit on a single machine and don't need distributed systems.
quotes.key_insight: By combining state-of-the-art query processing techniques (vectorized execution and morsel-driven parallelism) with a relentless focus on usability, DuckDB proves that academic rigor and practical impact are not opposing forces but complementary ones.

quotes.quote: Little did we know that this would create a ginormous Amazon bill, because we are now running at something like two terabytes a day of extension downloads.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen opens DuckCon #4 by sharing DuckDB's explosive adoption metrics, including 5 million monthly extension installs, half a million unique website visitors, and a rise to #45 on the DB-Engines ranking. He announces the upcoming 0.10 release and outlines the path to 1.0, which will follow a 'Snow Duck' philosophy inspired by Apple's Snow Leopard—focusing entirely on stability with no new features. The centerpiece of the 1.0 vision is backwards-compatible database file format, which Hannes positions as a potential new de facto standard for storing tabular data. Mark Raasveldt then details 0.10's features including multi-database attach for Postgres/MySQL, a 3x faster CSV reader, fixed-length arrays, unified memory manager, ALP floating point compression, and storage format improvements.
quotes.key_insight: DuckDB's path to 1.0 prioritizes stability and backwards-compatible file format permanence over new features, aiming to become the de facto standard for storing tabular data.

quotes.quote: We are just very grateful to Cloudflare to not charge us for the traffic.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen and Mark Raasveldt present the State of the Duck at DuckCon #4, covering DuckDB's explosive growth (5 million extension installs per month, 2TB/day of downloads, half a million unique web visitors) and the roadmap toward version 1.0. The release strategy involves a 'Snow Duck' approach inspired by Apple's Snow Leopard—no new features, just stability and robustness. A major milestone is the introduction of backwards-compatible database file format, positioning DuckDB as a potential de facto format for storing tabular data. New features in v0.10 include multi-database attach for Postgres/MySQL, a 3x faster CSV reader, fixed-length arrays, unified memory manager, secret manager, and ALP floating point compression. The talk also covers the team's lean operational philosophy, noting they lack VC funding and maintain a tiny DevRel team.
quotes.key_insight: DuckDB's path to 1.0 prioritizes stability and backwards-compatible file format over new features, betting that a trustworthy, forever-readable data format is more valuable than feature velocity.

quotes.quote: We were kind of building our system that was really, like, proudly single node and proudly in process.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen discusses the origins of DuckDB, which started from observing that big data tools were overkill for most users' actual needs. He explains DuckDB's key architectural decisions: single-node execution and in-process design, which were controversial in 2018 but proved correct. He emphasizes that developer experience is paramount, describing how the team invested heavily in things like CSV parsing rather than just query optimization. He also discusses the partnership model with MotherDuck as separate companies, allowing DuckDB Labs to focus purely on building the best database without commercial pressures.
quotes.key_insight: Most real-world data problems involve modest data sizes that are poorly served by distributed systems, and building a single-node in-process database with obsessive attention to developer experience fills an enormous gap.

quotes.quote: Maybe these petabyte sort of data sets are actually quite rare. And the people's real problems are like some couple of hundred megabytes of CSV files. And there's absolutely nothing out there in terms of software that can support that.

quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen discusses the origins of DuckDB, which started from observing that big data tools were overkill for most practitioners who work with modest datasets. He explains DuckDB's key architectural decisions: single-node processing and in-process execution, which were considered heretical in 2018 but have proven correct. Hannes emphasizes that DuckDB's success comes from obsessive focus on developer experience rather than just query performance, including investing a PhD-level effort into building the best CSV parser. He describes the unique two-company partnership between DuckDB Labs (open source database) and MotherDuck (managed service), which preserves DuckDB's independence from commercial pressures.
quotes.key_insight: DuckDB's success stems from recognizing that most data problems are small-scale and that obsessing over developer experience—from installation to CSV parsing—matters more than raw query performance.

quotes.quote: Record-keeping predates literature by about 1,000 years. So we've been making tables 1,000 years before we have the first text.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents DuckDB as the answer to the long-standing frustration that databases are slow and difficult to use. He demonstrates how traditional databases like Postgres require painful setup and CSV ingestion compared to data science tools, then shows how DuckDB eliminates this friction as an in-process analytical database. He argues that single-node computing is increasingly sufficient for virtually all datasets, backing this with a thought experiment about data creation rates versus hardware improvements, and demonstrates DuckDB outperforming a 1,024-core Spark cluster on a single machine.
quotes.key_insight: Single-node compute is growing faster than useful data creation, making distributed big data systems unnecessary for 99% of workloads, and DuckDB's in-process architecture proves databases can be neither slow nor frustrating.

quotes.quote: Record-keeping predates literature by about 1,000 years. So we've been making tables 1,000 years before we have the first text.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents DuckDB as the answer to the longstanding frustration that databases are slow and difficult to use for data analysis. He demonstrates how traditional databases like Postgres create painful workflows for simple CSV ingestion, while DuckDB offers zero-setup, in-process analytics with automatic type inference and blazing performance. He argues that single-node computing power is growing faster than data volumes, making distributed systems like Spark unnecessary for 99% of use cases. He showcases DuckDB's integration with R (duckplyr) and Python (IBIS), its atomic file format with views and macros, and the upcoming community extensions ecosystem.
quotes.key_insight: Single-node compute is growing faster than useful data volumes, making frustrating distributed systems unnecessary — what analysts need is a database that prioritizes user experience over enterprise sales.

quotes.quote: If your data fits in memory, there is no advantage of putting it into database. It will only be slower and more frustrating. And that really hurt our feelings.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents DuckDB's origin story and rationale at PyData Amsterdam 2023. He explains how a provocative quote from Hadley Wickham about databases being 'slower and more frustrating' motivated him and co-founder Mark Raasveldt to rethink the database user experience. DuckDB was designed as an in-process analytical database (like SQLite but for analytics) that eliminates installation friction, server management, and data transfer overhead. He demonstrates zero-copy querying of Pandas DataFrames and previews DuckSpark, a compatibility layer that runs PySpark scripts on DuckDB.
quotes.key_insight: The key innovation of DuckDB was recognizing that database frustration stems not from the engine itself but from the end-to-end user experience — and that in-process architecture eliminates most of that friction while preserving performance.

quotes.quote: It's the culmination of decades of research in data management systems. There's like tons of PhD theses whose results have gone into the concepts that make DuckDB fast.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents DuckDB's origin story and rationale at PyData Amsterdam 2023. He explains how a provocative quote from Hadley Wickham about databases being 'slower and more frustrating' motivated him and co-founder Mark Raasveldt to rethink the database user experience. DuckDB was designed as an in-process analytical database (like SQLite but for analytics) that eliminates installation friction, server management, and data transfer overhead. He demonstrates zero-copy querying of Pandas DataFrames and previews DuckSpark, a compatibility layer that runs PySpark scripts on DuckDB.
quotes.key_insight: The key innovation of DuckDB was recognizing that database frustration stems not from the engine itself but from the end-to-end user experience — and that in-process architecture eliminates most of that friction while preserving performance.

quotes.quote: Simple doesn't mean dumb. Simple means can also be fast.

quotes.quote: If your data fits in memory, there is no advantage of putting it into database. It will only be slower and more frustrating. And that really hurt our feelings.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents the rationale behind DuckDB at PyData Amsterdam 2023, explaining how it emerged from frustration with traditional database systems at CWI (where Python was also invented). He argues that databases were frustrating because of installation, maintenance, and data transfer overhead, but had valuable features like query optimization and persistence. DuckDB solves this by being an in-process database that runs within your existing Python, R, or Java process, eliminating the server setup entirely. He demonstrates DuckDB's ability to directly read Pandas DataFrames and Arrow tables without data copying, and previews DuckSpark, a compatibility layer that lets existing PySpark code run on DuckDB.
quotes.key_insight: The database industry's obsession with engine performance blinded it to the end-to-end user experience — DuckDB's in-process architecture eliminates the installation, maintenance, and data transfer friction that made databases frustrating, while preserving decades of research in query optimization and efficiency.

quotes.quote: Nobody had looked at sort of the end to end user experience of data systems. Everybody had been sort of hyper focused on the engine.

quotes.quote: What was happening is that there were people using very big iron systems like Hadoop to solve fairly small data problems.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents DuckDB at the Data + AI Summit 2023, explaining how he and Mark Raasveldt identified a fundamental mismatch in the data world: people were using massive distributed systems to solve relatively small data problems. He argues that the separation of storage and compute, combined with efficient formats like Parquet, means compute can scale down dramatically—often to a single laptop. DuckDB was built as an in-process analytical database with no dependencies, a 20MB footprint, and MIT licensing. He demonstrates querying a billion-row dataset in 160ms and 90GB of Parquet data in under 10 seconds on a MacBook, making the case that client-server architecture has been unquestioned since 1984 but is unnecessary for most analytical workloads.
quotes.key_insight: Most analytical data problems are far smaller than the infrastructure people use to solve them, and an in-process database on a laptop can replace cluster-scale systems for the majority of exploration and analysis work.

The separation of storage and compute, combined with efficient formats like Parquet, means compute can scale down dramatically—often to a single laptop. quotes.key_insight: The separation of storage and compute combined with efficient data formats means most analytical workloads can scale down to a laptop, making the decades-old client-server database paradigm unnecessary for the majority of data problems.

quotes.quote: Has anyone ever managed to set a Postgres instance for example in less than 5 minutes to read a parquet file?
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents DuckDB at EuroPython 2023, explaining how it combines the ease of use of DataFrame libraries with the analytical power of traditional database systems. He describes DuckDB's origins at CWI Amsterdam, inspired by SQLite's embedded approach but designed for analytics rather than transactions. The talk covers DuckDB's key technical advantages: columnar storage, vectorized execution, query optimization, parallelism, and beyond-memory execution. He demonstrates DuckDB's Python integration, showing it outperforming Pandas and PySpark on analytical queries while maintaining a tiny 16MB footprint.
quotes.key_insight: DuckDB bridges the gap between easy-to-use DataFrame libraries and powerful analytical databases by running an academically rigorous query engine in-process with zero-copy Python integration.

quotes.quote: Something that's super cool about the way we've done it is that you can create your own database type. So it's actually like a pluggable database architecture.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents the State of the Duck at DuckCon #3, covering DuckDB releases 0.7 and 0.8 from the preceding four months. Key features include multi-database attach support with pluggable storage backends, parallel JSON reading, hive-partition writes, pivot/unpivot operations, and as-of joins. He previews version 0.9 plans including out-of-core hash aggregation, compressed materialization, Apache Iceberg support, and better cloud platform integration. Adoption metrics show 1.3 million Python installs per month and 10K GitHub stars.
quotes.key_insight: DuckDB's architecture is evolving to handle datasets larger than memory through out-of-core operators and compressed materialization while maintaining its core philosophy of simplicity and pluggable extensibility.

quotes.quote: Something we always try to do is try to make things easy to use so that while you don't bother us, you don't ask any questions to us.

quotes.quote: It seemed insane—two people writing a database management system when these typically require hundreds of people over ten years. But I tend to leap without looking sometimes.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen describes how DuckDB was created at CWI in Amsterdam as an in-process analytical database, born from frustration that academic database research wasn't having real-world impact. He explains how talking to practitioners revealed that end-to-end experience mattered more than raw algorithm speed, and how restrictive IT environments shaped DuckDB's zero-dependency design. The conversation covers how Jordan Tigani discovered DuckDB and founded MotherDuck to commercialize it, with Hannes noting that Jordan's credibility from BigQuery validated the scale-up approach they'd been ridiculed for. Hannes reflects on the transition from academic researcher to running DuckDB Labs.
quotes.key_insight: DuckDB succeeded by focusing on the end-to-end data experience rather than raw query speed, and its scale-up philosophy — initially dismissed by the industry — was validated when practitioners with big-data credentials confirmed that most real-world data isn't actually big.

quotes.quote: I developed an uncanny ability to run things without Docker and a deep hatred for dependencies.

quotes.quote: We noticed great ideas existed but weren't having real-world impact. People weren't using state-of-the-art solutions.
quotes.summary: In this podcast interview, Hannes Mühleisen and Jordan Tigani discuss the origin of DuckDB and how MotherDuck was formed to commercialize it. Hannes explains how DuckDB emerged from academic research at CWI when he and Mark Raasveldt noticed state-of-the-art database ideas weren't reaching practitioners, largely due to deployment friction and dependency management. Jordan describes discovering DuckDB through benchmarks and recognizing that most data workloads don't require distributed systems. The conversation covers their rapid partnership formation, the scale-up vs scale-out debate, and the deliberate choice to keep DuckDB open while building a complementary commercial cloud product.
quotes.key_insight: DuckDB's success came from rejecting the distributed systems orthodoxy, eliminating deployment friction through zero dependencies, and focusing on the complete end-to-end user experience rather than raw query performance.

quotes.quote: It turns out that they weren't using databases at all, right? They were storing their data in CSV files.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen recounts the origin story of DuckDB, which grew out of research at CWI in the Netherlands. After engaging with R community data practitioners, he discovered they avoided databases entirely despite working with large datasets, due to slow client protocols and the burden of managing database servers. A prototype called MonoDB Lite proved the concept of in-process analytical databases but hit architectural limits, leading Hannes and Mark Raasveldt to build DuckDB from scratch—a multi-year effort that created an entirely new class of database system.
quotes.key_insight: DuckDB was born from discovering that data practitioners refused to use databases due to client-server complexity, revealing an entirely missing category: the in-process analytical database.

quotes.key_insight: DuckDB was born from discovering that data practitioners refused to use databases due to client-server complexity, revealing an entirely missing category: the in-process analytical database.

quotes.quote: One of our goals is to basically be able to squeeze as much out of a single machine, out of the laptop as possible, so that you can always get your queries running without having to resort to any sort of distributed system setup that's very complex to set up, very complex to run.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen and Mark Raasveldt present the State of the Duck at DuckCon #2, covering DuckDB's progress over the past year. Major achievements include persistent indexes, parallel data loading, advanced lightweight compression algorithms, out-of-core query execution for joins and sorting, and significant storage format improvements. DuckDB Labs has grown to 15 staff in Amsterdam, with adoption reaching one million Python installs per month. The talk outlines upcoming features including multi-database support, JSON reading, Hive partitioned writes, and plans for storage format finalization and V1.0.
quotes.key_insight: DuckDB's strategy of squeezing maximum performance from a single machine through lightweight compression, out-of-core execution, and pervasive parallelism eliminates the complexity of distributed systems for the vast majority of analytical workloads.

quotes.quote: One of our goals is to basically be able to squeeze as much out of a single machine, out of the laptop as possible. So that you can always get your queries running without having to resort to any sort of like distributed system setup that's very complex to set up, very complex to run.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen and Mark Raasveldt present the State of the Duck at DuckCon #2, reviewing DuckDB's progress over the past ten months across three major releases (0.4, 0.5, 0.6). The talk covers significant improvements in storage compression, out-of-core query processing, parallelism, and new SQL features. Hannes discusses DuckDB Labs growing to 15 staff in an Amsterdam office, DuckDB reaching one million Python installs per month, and the governance structure with the non-profit DuckDB Foundation ensuring the MIT license. The roadmap focuses on storage format finalization for V1.0, remaining out-of-core operators, partition awareness, and Lakehouse format support.
quotes.key_insight: DuckDB's strategy of maximizing single-machine performance through lightweight compression, out-of-core processing, and pervasive parallelism aims to eliminate the need for complex distributed systems, while its non-profit foundation governance ensures the open-source project remains independent of commercial pressures.

quotes.quote: DuckDB is very different there because it's a database as a library. We call it the in-process OLAP. And it's the first one because we kind of invented this as a class of systems.
quotes.summary: Hannes Mühleisen explains DuckDB as the first in-process OLAP database, designed to bring analytical SQL processing directly into applications like Python without a client-server architecture. He describes how DuckDB's vectorized execution engine processes data in chunks of 1,024 values to optimize CPU cache usage and amortize interpretation overhead. He contrasts DuckDB's single-node efficiency against distributed systems like Spark, arguing that most datasets fit on a laptop and distributed coordination often dominates execution time. He also discusses DuckDB's WebAssembly compilation for browser-based analytics, the 'never give up, never surrender' out-of-memory project, and the bootstrapped business model of DuckDB Labs.
quotes.key_insight: The database industry's obsession with distributed systems has caused it to neglect single-node performance so badly that an in-process analytical engine on a laptop can outperform clusters costing 32 times as much.

quotes.quote: People for the last 20 or so years have been working really hard on building distributed execution engines. And in my opinion, people have been neglecting the single node execution performance a lot. As a matter of fact, they've been neglecting it so much that single node execution has actually become slower.
